Whereas it is expedient for the security of the inhabitants, the suppression of vice and licentiousness, the preservation of peace ... I do therefore constitute and appoint Joseph Galloway, Esq; superintendant general, assisted by three magistrates of the police, to be hereafter appointed with powers and authority to make such orders and regulations from time to time, as may most effectually promote the salutary ends above mentioned ..

For the suppression of vice and immorality, and to prevent the disorders and mischiefs which may happen in the army, and among the inhabitants of the city of Philadelphia ... I do hereby order and direct, that no person or persons whatever shall keep any public inn, tavern, ale-house, tipling-house or dram-shop, or sell any rum or spirits ... unless such person shall have first obtained ... a permit or licence ..

subject: International law
first_sentence: THE FACTS.-The Companhia de Radio Internacional do Brasil, a corporation with its head office in Rio de Janeiro, filed a suit against the Federal Government in order to secure the reimbursement of a certain sum, plus interest and costs, in respect of excise tax levied from the Company.

THE FACTS.-A Financial Agreement concluded in Rome on April 17, 1947, between the Government of the United Kingdom and the Italian Government provided that the Custodian of Enemy Property should transfer Italian property which he held to the Italian Government to be paid into a special account with the Bank of England for the payment of debts in the United Kingdom which were chargeable on Italian property.
THE FACTS.-This was an action by a holder of debentures of the Danube-Save-Adriatic Railway Company against four persons as representing the Committee of Debenture Holders of that Company.
THE FACTS.-The appellants were an Order of Chivalry founded in Jerusalem in 1048 A.D.

THE FACTS.-One of the plaintiffs brought an action against the Director, Internal Revenue Service, under the Declaratory Judgment Act (62 Stat. 964, 28 U.S.C. 2201, 2202), to assert immunity from a substantial part of the United States income tax assessed against him for the year 1949 and to enjoin collection of the tax.

THE FACTS.-This was an appeal by the Public Prosecutor against a decision of a lower Court acquitting Cot-Riera, a Spanish national, on a charge of having kept a pastry-shop without possessing the permit entitling an alien to engage in commerce.

THE FACTS.-The appellant was an Armenian refugee whose action for reinstatement in a dwelling-house occupied by the cross-appellant, a French citizen, had been dismissed by the Court of first instance, on the ground that the relevant provisions of the Law of September 1, 1948, which would have given the appellant a right to reinstatement if he had been a French citizen, were not applicable to aliens.
